text.title: WCAG Severity Rubric Core Rules
text.summary: Five hard rules for triaging accessibility audit findings reproducibly across roles.

rule.statement: Every finding is scored on 3 axes: (a) WCAG level (A=3 / AA=2 / AAA=1), (b) user impact (3=task-block / 2=degrades / 1=annoyance / 0=cosmetic), (c) AT population affected (3=screen-reader OR keyboard-only / 2=low-vision OR cognitive / 1=color OR motion / 0=none). Severity = max-impact mapping defined in section "Mapping table".

rationale.source: Deque axe-core impact levels; WebAIM evaluation methodology

rule.rationale: Single-axis scoring (severity = WCAG level) overweights paperwork criteria and underweights actual user pain. Three-axis scoring forces the triager to consider real impact. Deque's axe-core uses a similar 4-tier impact (minor / moderate / serious / critical) grounded in user research.

rule.statement: "Blocker" severity is reserved for findings that prevent a target user from completing a primary user journey (signup, checkout, contact form, content read). Not "WCAG level A violation". A heading-level skip on a marketing page is not a blocker. Missing button label on Pay button IS a blocker.

rationale.source: WebAIM Million 2024 - 96% of homepages have WCAG violations

rule.rationale: If "blocker" includes every WCAG-A violation, 96% of products have 50+ blockers and the rubric is useless. Blocker = "cannot complete the job" makes the tier actionable. The legal compliance lens (every WCAG-A is a blocker for ADA Title II / EAA) is handled separately via the compliance methodology — this rubric is for engineering triage.

rule.statement: Before severity is finalized, the finding must be validated with the actual AT it claims to affect: screen-reader findings tested with NVDA or VoiceOver; keyboard findings tested without a mouse; cognitive findings reviewed by a person with the relevant lived experience or a domain expert. Unvalidated AT claims default to "moderate" max.
rule.rationale: Automated tools (axe, WAVE, Lighthouse) flag what they CAN flag; many findings are false positives without AT validation. Pushing every automated "critical" to blocker burns sprint budget on non-issues. AT validation is the empirical gate that separates real impact from theoretical impact.

rule.statement: Each severity tier has an SLA: blocker = fix before next deploy (hot-fix if in prod), serious = within 1 sprint, moderate = within 1 quarter, minor = next major refactor or close-as-wontfix with rationale. SLAs are written into the audit report and the team's Definition of Done.

rationale.source: ISO 30071-1 (digital inclusion management); ThoughtWorks tech radar a11y

rule.rationale: Without SLAs the moderate/minor bucket becomes a graveyard. Quantifying time-to-fix forces prioritization. The "minor with rationale" close is essential — without a wontfix path, every audit grows the backlog forever and team morale collapses.

rule.statement: For every 50 findings triaged, a second reviewer (or the audit author) re-scores 10% sampled at random. Severity must match in ≥80% of samples or the rubric is re-calibrated. This catches drift, batch-fatigue, and over-confidence in automated impact scores.

rationale.source: Inter-rater reliability practice; Deque internal QA process

rule.rationale: Triage drift is real — by finding 80 of 200, severity scoring is noticeably looser than at finding 5. Sampling forces a calibration checkpoint. 80% match is the industry-standard kappa threshold for reliable rubrics.

rule.statement: Skip WCAG Severity Rubric when any Skip-If condition holds. Do NOT produce an artefact; surface the gap to the requester.
rule.rationale: A methodology applied outside its scope produces low-signal artefacts that pollute the artefact store; the decision tree needs a clean terminator.

section.title: Mapping table (3-axis → severity)
section.code:
Compute s = wcag_level + user_impact + at_population
s ≥ 8 → blocker (e.g., WCAG-A + task-block + screen-reader) s = 6-7 → serious (e.g., AA + degrades + screen-reader OR A + task-block + low-vision) s = 4-5 → moderate s ≤ 3 → minor
Override: if user_impact == 3 (task-block) AND at_population ≥ 2, severity = blocker regardless of WCAG level (covers AAA blockers on the primary journey).

example.title: Finding triaged with full 3-axis score
example.code: { "finding_id": "F-042", "wcag_sc": "4.1.2 Name, Role, Value", "wcag_level": 3, "user_impact": 3, "at_population": 3, "axes_sum": 9, "severity": "blocker", "at_validation": "tested with NVDA 2024.4 + Firefox 128 — button announces as 'group' not 'button'", "location": "checkout/PayButton.tsx:line 47", "sla": "fix before next deploy" }
example.note: Illustrates r1 (3-axis), r2 (blocker = task-block on checkout), r3 (AT-validated).

example.title: Everything-p1 triage
example.code:
Auditor marks all 200 findings as severity=blocker because "WCAG compliance is critical".
Problem: violates r2 — blocker must mean task-block. Team capacity is ~10 blockers per sprint; backlog will never drain. PM loses trust in the rubric; findings get re-bucketed informally; rubric dies. Repair: re-triage on 3 axes. Expect typical distribution: 5-10% blocker, 20-30% serious, 40-50% moderate, 15-25% minor.
example.note: Illustrates the most common rubric-killing pattern.
